claimed_contribution: The paper provides the first systematic Monte Carlo comparison of covariance structure analysis, partial least squares, and generalized structured component analysis for SEM parameter recovery.

It introduces and explains generalized structured component analysis for marketing researchers and contrasts its model specification and estimation characteristics with the two traditional approaches.

It offers practice-oriented recommendations on method choice based on recovery performance, including generalized structured component analysis as an alternative to partial least squares and as preferable to covariance structure analysis when correct specification is uncertain.
context: The study is methodological and targets marketing/applied researchers using latent variable structural equation models. The simulation uses a model with three latent variables and four observed indicators per latent variable, including conditions with omitted cross-loadings and an added path.
design_and_data: The authors run a Monte Carlo simulation with four factors: approach (covariance structure analysis, partial least squares, generalized structured component analysis), sample size (N = 100, 200, 300, 400, 500), model specification (correct vs misspecified), and distribution (normal vs nonnormal). They generate 500 datasets per condition, for 10,000 datasets total; nonnormal data are created via Fleishman transformation with skewness 1.25 and kurtosis 3.75, and data generation is based on an implied covariance matrix from a covariance-structure formulation. Each dataset is fit with all three approaches, and recovery of loadings, path coefficients, and their standard errors is evaluated using mean absolute differences plus relative bias, standard deviation, and mean square error. Nonconverged or improper solutions are excluded; standard errors for generalized structured component analysis and partial least squares are obtained by bootstrap with 100 resamples.
main_findings: Model specification was the only meaningful condition for differentiating the three approaches in parameter recovery.

When the model was correctly specified, covariance structure analysis generally recovered loadings and path coefficients better than generalized structured component analysis and partial least squares, but under misspecification generalized structured component analysis recovered them better.

Partial least squares showed relatively inferior parameter recovery, especially when cross-loadings were present, while generalized structured component analysis generally provided the most accurate standard error estimates under both specification levels.
one_line_summary: Using a 10,000-dataset Monte Carlo study, the paper finds that covariance structure analysis is best under correct specification, generalized structured component analysis is more robust under misspecification and for standard error estimation, and partial least squares is generally weaker, especially with cross-loadings.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces a three-approach comparison that earlier SEM simulation work had not provided, because prior studies largely contrasted covariance structure analysis with partial least squares. It also builds on prior evidence of covariance structure analysis sensitivity to misspecification by showing a relative advantage of generalized structured component analysis when models are misspecified.
research_gap: The authors state that despite the availability of covariance structure analysis and partial least squares and the recent introduction of generalized structured component analysis, no prior study had examined the relative performance of all three approaches. As a result, guidance on which approach better recovers parameters under common SEM conditions was lacking.
research_question: How do covariance structure analysis, partial least squares, and generalized structured component analysis compare in recovering SEM loadings, path coefficients, and their standard errors? The paper also asks whether relative performance changes across sample size, data distribution, and correct versus misspecified models.
